Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>先做第 2 項</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-06</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
兩題的答案：**1. 可以，已經有 `/model` 指令**；**2. 只有「靜態檢查」，沒有真正的可用性/額度檢查** — 詳細如下。

## 1. 切換 AI Model — 已支援 ✅

`src/commands/model.ts` 已實作 `/model` 指令：

- **`/model`**（不帶參數）— 顯示當前模型 + 全部可用列表，未設 API key 的會標 `⚠️ 未設定 API key`
- **`/model <名稱>`** — 切換 provider，支援的別名有：
  - `kiro` / `acp` → Kiro CLI (ACP)
  - `claude` / `anthropic` → Anthropic API
  - `gpt4o` / `openai` / `gpt` → OpenAI API
  - `gemini` / `google` → Google Gemini
  - `deepseek`、`groq` / `llama`、`local` / `ollama`

注意切換會呼叫 `sessions.switchProvider()` 並**重置該 chat 的對話歷史**（指令回覆也會先警告你）。

## 2. 檢查 Model 是否可用 — 目前只有淺層檢查 ⚠️

現況有兩層，但都碰不到「usage 到上限」這種情況：

- **`/model` 的 `isAvailable()`**（`model.ts:101`）— 只檢查 **API key 有沒有設定**，`acp` 和 `local` 甚至寫死回傳 `true`。key 存在但額度用完、key 失效，它照樣顯示可用。
- **啟動時 Doctor health check**（`src/doctor.ts`）— 檢查 ACP 指令在不在 PATH、llama.cpp server 有沒有起來、Redis、memory 目錄等，**沒有打任何 LLM API 驗證額度**。

另外我掃過 `src/provider/`，**沒有 429 / quota / rate-limit 的錯誤分類，也沒有額度爆掉時自動 fallback 到其他 provider 的機制**——目前如果某個 model 額度用完，你會在對話中直接收到錯誤訊息，然後要自己手動 `/model` 切走。

## 如果要補「真正的可用性檢查」，可行方向

1. **輕量 probe**：`/model` 列表時（或加一個 `/model check`）對各 provider 發一個極小請求（例如 `max_tokens: 1`），依回傳狀態標記 ✅ 可用 / ❌ 額度用盡或 key 失效。Anthropic/OpenAI 的 429 回應能直接區分 rate limit vs quota。
2. **錯誤攔截 + 提示**：在 provider adapter（`src/provider/adapters/*`）攔截 429/quota 錯誤，回覆時直接建議「此模型額度已滿，可用 `/model xxx` 切換」，甚至自動 fallback。
3. **Kiro (ACP) 的限制**：Kiro CLI 的訂閱用量沒有查詢 API，只能靠實際發 prompt 失敗時偵測，做不到事前查餘額。

如果你要的話，我可以實作其中一項（建議先做第 2 項，改動小、體感提升最直接）。

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{ Authorization: `Bearer ${this.cfg.apiKey}` } : {}),\\n      },\\n      body: JSON.stringify(body),\\n    });\\n\\n    if (!res.ok) {\\n      const text = await res.text().catch(() => \\\"\\\");\\n      throw new Error(`OpenAI API ${res.status}: ${text.slice(0, 500)}`);\\n    }\\n\\n    return this.parseSSE(res, opts.onText, opts.onThinking);\\n  }\\n\\n  private async parseSSE(\\n    res: Response,\\n    onText?: (chunk: string) => void,\\n    onThinking?: (chunk: string) => void,\\n  ): Promise<{ message: ChatMessage; stopReason: string }> {\\n    const reader = res.body!.getReader();\\n    const decoder = new TextDecoder();\\n    let buffer = \\\"\\\";\\n    let fullText = \\\"\\\";\\n    let fullReasoning = \\\"\\\";\\n    let toolCalls: Map<number, { id: string; name: string; args: string }> = new Map();\\n    let stopReason = \\\"end_turn\\\";\\n\\n    for (;;) {\\n      const { done, value } = await reader.read();\\n      if (done) break;\\n      buffer += decoder.decode(value, { stream: true });\\n\\n      const lines = buffer.split(\\\"\\\\n\\\");\\n      buffer = lines.pop()!; // keep incomplete line\\n\\n      for (const line of lines) {\\n        if (!line.startsWith(\\\"data: \\\")) continue;\\n        const data = line.slice(6).trim();\\n        if (data === \\\"[DONE]\\\") continue;\\n\\n        let parsed: any;\\n        try { parsed = JSON.parse(data); } catch { continue; }\\n\\n        const delta = parsed.choices?.[0]?.delta;\\n        const finishReason = parsed.choices?.[0]?.finish_reason;\\n\\n        if (finishReason) {\\n          stopReason = finishReason === \\\"tool_calls\\\" ? \\\"tool_use\\\" : finishReason;\\n        }\\n\\n        if (!delta) continue;\\n\\n        // Text content\\n        if (delta.content) {\\n          fullText += delta.content;\\n          onText?.(delta.content);\\n        }\\n\\n        // Reasoning/thinking content (qwen3, DeepSeek-R1, etc.)\\n        if (delta.reasoning) {\\n          fullReasoning += delta.reasoning;\\n          onThinking?.(delta.reasoning);\\n        }\\n\\n        // Tool calls (streamed incrementally)\\n        if (delta.tool_calls) {\\n          for (const tc of delta.tool_calls) {\\n            const idx = tc.index ?? The user is asking me to create a progress summary for the observed session. Let me analyze what happened:

1. The user said "先做第 2 項" (start with item 2)
2. Claude's response discussed two topics:
   - Topic 1: Whether model switching is supported - Answer: Yes, the `/model` command already exists
   - Topic 2: Whether there's a check for model availability - Answer: Only static checks exist, no real usage/quota checking

Claude investigated:
- The existing `/model` command implementation in `src/commands/model.ts`
- The model switching functionality and supported aliases (kiro, claude, gpt4o, gemini, deepseek, groq, local)
- The current availability checking mechanism (only checks if API keys are configured)
- The Doctor health check at startup in `src/doctor.ts`
- Provider adapters in `src/provider/` looking for quota/rate-limit handling

Claude learned:
- The `/model` command already supports switching between different AI providers
- Current availability checks are shallow - only verify API key existence, not actual quotas
- No 429/quota/rate-limit error handling exists in the codebase
- No automatic fallback mechanism when a provider runs out of quota

Completed work:
- This appears to be investigative work only - examining existing code to answer the user's questions
- No actual code changes were made

Next steps:
- Claude suggested implementing option 2: Error interception + prompting to catch 429/quota errors and suggest switching models
- The user said "先做第 2 項" which likely means "start with item 2" - referring to implementing the error handling feature

Let me write the summary focusing on what was investigated and learned, since this is primarily a discovery/investigation phase.

content.text:
```xml
<summary>
  <request>Investigate model switching capability and availability checking in the AI chat system</request>
  <investigated>Examined the existing `/model` command implementation in `src/commands/model.ts`, the `isAvailable()` checking logic, Doctor health checks in `src/doctor.ts`, and provider adapters in `src/provider/` for quota/rate-limit handling mechanisms</investigated>
  <learned>The system already has a `/model` command that supports switching between providers (Kiro/ACP, Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek, Groq, Ollama) with aliases. However, availability checking is shallow - it only verifies API key configuration, not actual quota or usage limits. The `isAvailable()` function returns true for ACP and local providers regardless of actual status. No 429/quota/rate-limit error classification exists in provider adapters, and there's no automatic fallback mechanism when a model hits quota limits. Users currently see raw error messages when quotas are exceeded and must manually switch providers using `/model`</learned>
  <completed>Completed investigation of model switching and availability checking capabilities. Identified gap: no real-time quota checking or automatic error handling for exhausted models</completed>
  <next_steps>User requested to implement option 2: add error interception in provider adapters to catch 429/quota errors, provide helpful suggestions to switch models, and potentially implement automatic fallback when a provider hits limits</next_steps>
  <notes>Three implementation approaches were proposed: (1) lightweight probe requests to check availability, (2) error interception with user prompts, (3) Kiro ACP limitation noted - no API to query subscription usage. Option 2 was recommended as it requires minimal changes with maximum UX improvement</notes>
</summary>
```

{ Authorization: `Bearer ${this.cfg.apiKey}` } : {}),\\n      },\\n      body: JSON.stringify(body),\\n    });\\n\\n    if (!res.ok) {\\n      const text = await res.text().catch(() => \\\"\\\");\\n      throw new Error(`OpenAI API ${res.status}: ${text.slice(0, 500)}`);\\n    }\\n\\n    return this.parseSSE(res, opts.onText, opts.onThinking);\\n  }\\n\\n  private async parseSSE(\\n    res: Response,\\n    onText?: (chunk: string) => void,\\n    onThinking?: (chunk: string) => void,\\n  ): Promise<{ message: ChatMessage; stopReason: string }> {\\n    const reader = res.body!.getReader();\\n    const decoder = new TextDecoder();\\n    let buffer = \\\"\\\";\\n    let fullText = \\\"\\\";\\n    let fullReasoning = \\\"\\\";\\n    let toolCalls: Map<number, { id: string; name: string; args: string }> = new Map();\\n    let stopReason = \\\"end_turn\\\";\\n\\n    for (;;) {\\n      const { done, value } = await reader.read();\\n      if (done) break;\\n      buffer += decoder.decode(value, { stream: true });\\n\\n      const lines = buffer.split(\\\"\\\\n\\\");\\n      buffer = lines.pop()!; // keep incomplete line\\n\\n      for (const line of lines) {\\n        if (!line.startsWith(\\\"data: \\\")) continue;\\n        const data = line.slice(6).trim();\\n        if (data === \\\"[DONE]\\\") continue;\\n\\n        let parsed: any;\\n        try { parsed = JSON.parse(data); } catch { continue; }\\n\\n        const delta = parsed.choices?.[0]?.delta;\\n        const finishReason = parsed.choices?.[0]?.finish_reason;\\n\\n        if (finishReason) {\\n          stopReason = finishReason === \\\"tool_calls\\\" ? \\\"tool_use\\\" : finishReason;\\n        }\\n\\n        if (!delta) continue;\\n\\n        // Text content\\n        if (delta.content) {\\n          fullText += delta.content;\\n          onText?.(delta.content);\\n        }\\n\\n        // Reasoning/thinking content (qwen3, DeepSeek-R1, etc.)\\n        if (delta.reasoning) {\\n          fullReasoning += delta.reasoning;\\n          onThinking?.(delta.reasoning);\\n        }\\n\\n        // Tool calls (streamed incrementally)\\n        if (delta.tool_calls) {\\n          for (const tc of delta.tool_calls) {\\n            const idx = tc.index ?? content.thinking:
The user is reading provider adapter files to understand how errors are currently handled. They've looked at:
1. `src/provider/types.ts` - The provider interface and type definitions
2. `src/provider/adapters/anthropic.ts` - Anthropic API adapter
3. `src/provider/adapters/openai-compat.ts` - OpenAI-compatible adapter

This is investigation work to understand the current error handling before implementing option 2 (error interception for quota/rate-limit errors). Looking at these files, I can see:

- Both adapters have basic error handling: they check `if (!res.ok)` and throw a generic error with the status code and response text
- There's no specific handling for 429 (rate limit) or quota errors
- There's no classification of error types
- The error messages are just thrown generically: `throw new Error(\`Anthropic API ${res.status}: ${text.slice(0, 500)}\`)`

This is discovery work - learning how the existing system works before making changes.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Provider adapter error handling lacks quota/rate-limit detection</title>
  <subtitle>Both Anthropic and OpenAI-compatible adapters use generic error handling without classifying quota or rate-limit failures</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Provider interface in types.ts defines prompt() method returning {stopReason: string} with no error classification</fact>
    <fact>AnthropicAdapter in anthropic.ts checks res.ok and throws generic Error with status code and response text slice</fact>
    <fact>OpenAICompatAdapter in openai-compat.ts uses identical error pattern: check res.ok then throw generic error message</fact>
    <fact>Neither adapter distinguishes HTTP 429 rate-limit errors from other failure types</fact>
    <fact>No structured error types exist for quota exhaustion, rate limiting, or authentication failures</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Investigation of provider adapters revealed that error handling is currently generic across all LLM providers. Both the Anthropic Messages API adapter (anthropic.ts) and the OpenAI-compatible adapter (openai-compat.ts) follow the same pattern: after making a fetch request to the LLM API, they check if the response is ok, and if not, throw a plain Error with the HTTP status code and up to 500 characters of the response text. There is no logic to detect or classify specific error conditions like 429 (rate limit exceeded), quota exhaustion, or invalid API keys. This means that when a model runs out of quota or hits rate limits, users receive raw error messages without helpful guidance to switch providers or retry later. The provider interface itself (types.ts) only specifies that prompt() returns a stopReason string, with no standard error taxonomy for the adapters to implement.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/provider/types.ts</file>
    <file>src/provider/adapters/anthropic.ts</file>
    <file>src/provider/adapters/openai-compat.ts</file>

content.thinking:
The user is looking at error handling in run-prompt.ts. I can see multiple catch blocks with various error handling patterns. Most of them just log errors and continue or queue messages. There doesn't seem to be any specific handling for quota/rate-limit errors from the LLM providers.

This is part of investigating where to add the error interception for option 2 - adding error interception in provider adapters to catch 429/quota errors and provide helpful suggestions to switch models.

Let me record this as a discovery about the current error handling flow in the main prompt execution loop.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Run-prompt error handling focuses on message delivery, not LLM API failures</title>
  <subtitle>Main prompt execution loop in run-prompt.ts handles Telegram delivery failures but lacks LLM-specific error classification</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>run-prompt.ts contains 40+ catch blocks primarily handling Telegram API failures for message editing and sending</fact>
    <fact>Error handling pattern uses best-effort fallbacks: edit fails → try direct send → queue message for later replay</fact>
    <fact>DirectProvider.prompt() calls are made without try-catch wrappers that distinguish LLM quota/rate-limit errors</fact>
    <fact>Generic catch blocks at lines 980, 990, 1009, 1038 handle provider errors but only log them without classification</fact>
    <fact>No error messages suggest switching models when quota exhausted or rate limits hit</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Investigation of the main prompt execution flow revealed that error handling is heavily focused on Telegram message delivery reliability rather than LLM provider failures. The file contains over 40 catch blocks implementing a best-effort message delivery pattern: if editing a message fails, try direct send; if that fails, queue it for later replay. However, when DirectProvider.prompt() throws errors from the LLM API adapters (Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible), they are caught by generic catch blocks that only log the error without examining the HTTP status code or error type. There is no logic to detect 429 rate-limit responses, quota exhaustion, or authentication failures, and no user-facing guidance to switch models using the /model command when these conditions occur. This means users see raw error messages without actionable suggestions.</narrative>

content.thinking:
This is showing the main error handling catch block in run-prompt.ts (lines 1389-1469). The key observations:

1. Line 1389-1390: The catch block casts the error to `Error & { code?: number; data?: unknown }`
2. Lines 1391-1399: It logs the error with code, message, and data fields
3. Lines 1400-1428: Special handling for code -32603 (ACP/kiro-cli specific error)
4. Line 1400: `let msg = `Error: ${e.message}`;` - This is where the error message is constructed for the user
5. Lines 1401-1428: Only special-case handling is for ACP errors (code -32603), checking for "prompt already in progress" or showing stderr
6. Lines 1429-1439: Send the error message to the user (via relay or telegram edit/reply)

Key finding: This is the MAIN error catch block for `session.client.prompt()` failures, but it only has special handling for ACP-specific errors (code -32603). There's NO detection of:
- HTTP 429 (rate limit)
- Quota exhaustion
- Authentication failures
- Any LLM API-specific errors

The error from Anthropic/OpenAI adapters comes through as a generic Error with a message like "Anthropic API 429: ..." or "OpenAI API 429: ...", but there's no parsing of that to provide helpful guidance like "This model's quota is exhausted, try /model to switch providers."

This confirms the gap: need to add intelligent error parsing here to detect quota/rate-limit errors and provide actionable suggestions.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Main error handler only has special logic for ACP errors, not LLM API failures</title>
  <subtitle>The catch block at line 1389 handles prompt failures but only detects ACP-specific code -32603, missing HTTP 429 and quota errors</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Main error handler at run-prompt.ts:1389 catches all session.client.prompt() failures into generic Error type</fact>
    <fact>Special-case logic only exists for code -32603 (ACP JSON-RPC internal errors) checking for "prompt already in progress" or showing stderr</fact>
    <fact>LLM API errors from Anthropic/OpenAI adapters arrive as generic Error messages like "Anthropic API 429: ..." without status code fields</fact>
    <fact>Error message construction at line 1400 defaults to "Error: {e.message}" without parsing HTTP status or suggesting provider switches</fact>
    <fact>No detection logic exists for HTTP 429 rate limits, quota exhaustion, or authentication failures from LLM providers</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The main error handling catch block in run-prompt.ts (starting at line 1389) is the final safety net for all prompt execution failures. It casts errors to a type with optional code and data fields, then logs and displays them to users. However, the only intelligent error detection is for ACP-specific failures (code -32603), where it checks for "prompt already in progress" messages or shows agent stderr. When Anthropic or OpenAI adapters throw errors due to rate limiting (HTTP 429) or quota exhaustion, they arrive as plain Error objects with messages like "Anthropic API 429: {response text}". The handler does not parse these messages to extract the HTTP status code, detect quota/rate-limit conditions, or provide actionable guidance such as suggesting users run /model to switch to a different provider. This is the key integration point where intelligent error classification should be added to improve the user experience when models hit limits.</narrative>

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>DirectProvider.prompt() passes adapter errors through without transformation</title>
  <subtitle>The prompt method calls runToolLoop with adapter and tools but does not catch or classify errors before they bubble up</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>DirectProvider.prompt() at line 105 calls runToolLoop() at line 124 within try-finally block that only manages _inflight flag</fact>
    <fact>No try-catch around runToolLoop means adapter errors (from anthropic.ts, openai-compat.ts) propagate directly to caller</fact>
    <fact>Return value only contains stopReason field, no error classification or metadata</fact>
    <fact>DirectProvider emits SessionUpdate events for streaming chunks but has no error event type</fact>
    <fact>Adapter errors thrown as generic Error objects reach run-prompt.ts unchanged without status codes or error types</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The DirectProvider.prompt() method in direct.ts is the integration point between the high-level session management and the low-level LLM adapters. It prepares messages with context compaction, then calls runToolLoop() which internally invokes the adapter's chat() method. The entire runToolLoop call is wrapped only in a try-finally block that manages the _inflight flag - there is no try-catch to intercept or transform errors from the adapters. This means when AnthropicAdapter or OpenAICompatAdapter throw errors due to HTTP 429 rate limits or quota exhaustion, those plain Error objects with messages like "Anthropic API 429: ..." propagate unchanged all the way up to the main error handler in run-prompt.ts. The Provider interface in types.ts also does not define error event types - only update, stderr, exit, and request events exist. This architecture makes DirectProvider a natural interception point where adapter errors could be caught, classified by HTTP status code, and either re-thrown as structured errors or emitted as error events.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/provider/direct.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

2. None of these calls have try-catch blocks around them

This confirms that adapter.chat() calls propagate errors directly without any interception or classification. This is the deepest level where adapter errors could be caught before they bubble up to DirectProvider.prompt() and then to run-prompt.ts.

This is important because it shows that:
- tool-loop.ts makes multiple adapter.chat() calls in different scenarios (context overflow, normal flow, tool loop break, max iterations)
- None of these calls catch or classify adapter errors
- Errors from HTTP 429, quota exhaustion, etc. pass through unchanged
- This is another potential interception point (though DirectProvider.prompt() might be cleaner)

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Tool-loop makes multiple adapter.chat() calls without error interception</title>
  <subtitle>Four adapter.chat() invocation sites in tool-loop.ts propagate all errors directly without try-catch or classification</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>tool-loop.ts calls adapter.chat() at lines 105, 111, 136, and 181 in different scenarios (context overflow, main loop, tool break, max iterations)</fact>
    <fact>None of the adapter.chat() calls have try-catch blocks wrapping them for error classification</fact>
    <fact>Only catch blocks in tool-loop.ts handle JSON.parse errors (lines 223, 353) and tool execution errors (line 270), not adapter errors</fact>
    <fact>Tool execution errors at line 270 are caught and converted to error messages returned as tool results, but adapter errors propagate unchanged</fact>
    <fact>All four adapter.chat() call sites would need modification to add consistent error handling at this layer</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The runToolLoop function in tool-loop.ts is the orchestration layer that manages the agent's turn-by-turn conversation with tool calling. It makes four distinct calls to adapter.chat() in different control flow scenarios: when context is nearly exhausted (line 105), during the normal tool-calling loop (line 111), when breaking out of the tool loop to get a text response (line 136), and when max iterations are reached (line 181). None of these call sites wrap adapter.chat() in try-catch blocks, meaning all errors from the underlying LLM adapters (Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible) propagate unchanged up to DirectProvider.prompt(). The only error handling in tool-loop.ts is for JSON parsing failures when deserializing tool arguments, and for tool execution failures which are caught, logged, and returned as error messages to the model. This multi-site invocation pattern means that adding error classification at the tool-loop level would require changes at four locations, while adding it at the DirectProvider.prompt() level (which wraps runToolLoop) would centralize the logic.</narrative>

2. It has a "rate-limit" category but maps to Telegram bot rate limit handling

5. It can use local LLM for classification or fall back to regex patterns

  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Error classifier exists but focuses on Telegram/shell errors, not LLM provider failures</title>
  <subtitle>error-classifier.ts provides category detection infrastructure with 14 patterns but none specifically for LLM quota exhaustion or authentication</subtitle>

  <narrative>Discovery of error-classifier.ts reveals that error classification infrastructure already exists in the codebase. The classifyError() function takes error text and returns a structured ErrorClassification object containing category, confidence score, and an optional suggested skill for handling. It supports 14 predefined error categories using regex pattern matching (or local LLM classification as a premium option), covering common failure modes like syntax errors, module resolution, permissions, network issues, and even specialized cases like Windows shell interactive prompts and Cocos editor IPC errors. Notably, there is a "rate-limit" category that matches HTTP 429 responses, but it maps to "ms-telegram-bot-rate-limit-survival" - a skill for handling Telegram Bot API rate limits, not LLM provider quota exhaustion. The existing categories focus on runtime, filesystem, network, and shell errors rather than LLM API-specific failures like authentication errors, quota exhaustion, or model unavailability. This infrastructure provides a natural extension point: new categories could be added for LLM provider errors with associated guidance to switch models using /model.</narrative>

    <fact>Each test verifies specific behaviors numbered 1-N in header comments explaining what's being tested</fact>
    <fact>check-atomic-json.mjs tests edge cases: ENOENT fallback, corrupt JSON handling, concurrent updates, tmp file cleanup</fact>
    <fact>Scripts run directly with node command, no test framework dependencies required</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Examination of check-atomic-json.mjs reveals the testing conventions used throughout the project's 51 check scripts. Each script is a standalone Node.js module that can be run directly with node, importing from the compiled dist/ directory (requiring tsc to be run first). The pattern is lightweight and consistent: a fresh() function handles test environment setup and cleanup using recursive rm/mkdir, while ok(name) and fail(name, detail) helper functions track results and provide visual feedback with emoji. Tests are structured as sequential verification steps, with header comments documenting exactly what behaviors are being checked (numbered 1-N). The check-atomic-json.mjs example tests edge cases like missing files, corrupt JSON, concurrent updates, and cleanup verification - demonstrating that these scripts are thorough smoke tests rather than just happy-path checks. This pattern would translate naturally to a check-provider-error-handling.mjs script that could verify rate-limit detection, quota error messages, and model switch suggestions by simulating or mocking HTTP 429 responses from LLM providers.</narrative>

body\\n * can distinguish \\\"plan scheduled for next turn\\\" from \\\"we're currently\\n * running one\\\" at the type level. */\\ntype ContinuationPlan = ContinuationContext;\\n\\nexport type RunPromptMode =\\n  | { kind: \\\"user\\\"; replyHeader?: string }\\n  | { kind: \\\"continuation\\\"; context: ContinuationContext }\\n  | { kind: \\\"meta\\\"; silent?: boolean }\\n  | { kind: \\\"relay\\\"; replyTo: string };\\n\\n// ─── Sticker auto-selection ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nconst EMOTION_REPRS: Record<string, string> = {\\n  hello: \\\"打招呼 你好 嗨 早安 晚安 greeting\\\",\\n  happy: \\\"開心 太好了 成功 完成了 恭喜 great\\\",\\n  thanks: \\\"謝謝 感謝 thank you 不客氣\\\",\\n  sorry: \\\"抱歉 對不起 不好意思 sorry\\\",\\n  agree: \\\"同意 沒問題 好的 了解 ok sure\\\",\\n  good: \\\"做得好 讚 很棒 nice well done\\\",\\n  clap: \\\"恭喜 太厲害了 完美 bravo\\\",\\n  love: \\\"喜歡 愛 太棒了 超讚\\\",\\n  sad: \\\"可惜 遺憾 不幸 unfortunately\\\",\\n  shock: \\\"驚訝 天啊 沒想到 wow unexpected\\\",\\n  ok: \\\"收到 了解 知道了 acknowledged\\\",\\n};\\nconst STICKER_AUTO_THRESHOLD = 0.65;\\nconst STICKER_AUTO_MAX_REPLY_LEN = 150;\\nlet emotionVectors: Map<string, Float32Array> | null = null;\\n\\nexport async function autoSelectSticker(reply: string): Promise<string | null> {\\n  if (reply.length > STICKER_AUTO_MAX_REPLY_LEN || reply.length < 5) return null;\\n  if (!emotionVectors) {\\n    emotionVectors = new Map();\\n    for (const [emotion, repr] of Object.entries(EMOTION_REPRS)) {\\n      const vec = await embedText(repr);\\n      if (vec) emotionVectors.set(emotion, vec);\\n    }\\n  }\\n  const queryVec = await embedText(reply);\\n  if (!queryVec) return null;\\n  let best = \\\"\\\";\\n  let bestScore = 0;\\n  for (const [emotion, vec] of emotionVectors) {\\n    const score = cosineSim(queryVec, vec);\\n    if (score > bestScore) { bestScore = score; best = emotion; }\\n  }\\n  return bestScore >= STICKER_AUTO_THRESHOLD ? best : null;\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Continuation timer onRateLimitRecovery: (fn: () => void | Promise<void>) => () => void;\\n  queuePendingMessage: (chatId: number, text: string) => void;\\n  handleRestart: (ctx: Context, opts?: { reason?: string }) => content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Created quota error classifier for LLM provider failures</title>
  <subtitle>New quota-error.ts module detects quota exhaustion and rate limiting with user-friendly model switch suggestions</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Created src/provider/quota-error.ts with classifyQuotaError() and quotaHint() functions</fact>
    <fact>Distinguishes two error kinds: "quota-exhausted" (non-transient) and "rate-limited" (transient/overload)</fact>
    <fact>QUOTA_PATTERNS array matches 7 patterns: insufficient quota, exceeded quota, insufficient balance, API 402, usage limit, monthly limit</fact>
    <fact>RATE_PATTERNS array matches 6 patterns: API 429, rate_limit, too many requests, RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED, API 529, overloaded</fact>
    <fact>quotaHint() returns localized Chinese messages suggesting /model command to switch providers when limits hit</fact>
    <fact>Designed to work with both Direct adapter error format ("Anthropic API 429: ...") and ACP stderr output</fact>
    <fact>Classification prioritizes quota exhaustion over rate limiting when patterns overlap</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented the first component of improved LLM error handling by creating a dedicated quota-error.ts module in the provider directory. This module provides two main functions: classifyQuotaError() which categorizes error text into "quota-exhausted" or "rate-limited" types, and quotaHint() which generates user-facing guidance. The classification uses two regex pattern arrays covering provider-specific error messages from Anthropic (credit balance, 529 overload), OpenAI (insufficient quota, 429), Gemini (RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED), DeepSeek (insufficient balance, 402), and Kiro ACP (usage limit, monthly limit). When quota is exhausted, the hint message directs users to the /model command to view and switch providers, noting that switching will reset the conversation. For rate limiting, it suggests either waiting or switching models. The module is designed as the interception point for run-prompt.ts's catch block, where all provider errors converge, and can parse both the formatted errors from Direct adapters ("Provider API StatusCode: message") and ACP stderr tails.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/provider/quota-error.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The user has edited run-prompt.ts to add quota error detection. They're adding:
1. A call to quotaHint() that combines error message and stderr tail
2. If a hint is returned, append it to the error message shown to users

This is integrating the quota-error.ts module into the main error handler. However, I notice they haven't imported the quotaHint function yet - they just added the call. Let me wait to see if an import is added next.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Integrated quota error hints into main prompt error handler</title>
  <subtitle>Modified run-prompt.ts error catch block to detect quota/rate-limit failures and append user-friendly model switch guidance</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Added quotaHint() call at line 1418 in run-prompt.ts main error handler catch block</fact>
    <fact>Function receives combined error message and session.stderrTail to detect both Direct adapter and ACP errors</fact>
    <fact>When quota or rate-limit errors detected, hint message appended to error output shown to user</fact>
    <fact>Integration point is after ACP-specific error handling (code -32603) but before relay/placeholder message delivery</fact>
    <fact>Hint appears in both relay error notices and Telegram visible error messages</fact>
    <fact>Error classification runs on every prompt failure regardless of provider type (ACP, Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Integrated the quota error detection module into the main prompt execution error handler in run-prompt.ts. The catch block at line 1389 already handles all prompt failures from any provider. After the existing ACP-specific error logic (which checks for code -32603 and formats kiro-cli errors), three new lines were added: call quotaHint() with both the error message and session.stderrTail combined, check if a hint was returned, and if so append it to the user-facing error message. This integration point captures errors from all providers because DirectProvider.prompt() throws directly into this catch block, and ACP errors also arrive here with stderr context in session.stderrTail. The combined text input to quotaHint() ensures patterns like "API 429" from Direct adapters and "usage limit" from ACP stderr are both matched. When a quota or rate-limit pattern is detected, users now see actionable guidance pointing them to the /model command, rather than just a raw error string.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

(ab7.text ? `${ab7.text}\\\\n\\\\n${scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\")}` : scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\"))\\n    : ab7.text;\\n  const askKeyboard = buildAskKeyboard(ab7.askTokens);\\n\\n  // Text delivery (chunked). === chunks.length - 1;\\n      const pageTag = chunks.length > 1 ? `(${i + 1}/${chunks.length})\\\\n` : \\\"\\\";\\n      const text = pageTag + chunks[i];\\n      const extra = isLast && askKeyboard  } catch (err) {\\n        console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] chunk send failed, queueing for replay:`, err);\\n        queuePendingMessage(chatId, text);\\n      }\\n      if (!isLast) await new content.thinking:
The user has created a comprehensive smoke test script for the quota error detection feature. This follows the project's testing pattern where each feature has a check-*.mjs script in the scripts/ directory. The test covers:

1. All major LLM providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepSeek, Gemini, Kiro)
2. Both error types (quota-exhausted and rate-limited)
3. Priority rules (quota patterns take precedence over rate-limit when both match)
4. False positive prevention (normal errors don't trigger hints)
5. Message content validation (hints contain /model and appropriate Chinese text)

This is a well-structured test following the same pattern as check-atomic-json.mjs we saw earlier - using ok/fail helpers, structured test cases, and clear verification steps.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Created comprehensive smoke test for quota error detection</title>
  <subtitle>New check-quota-hint.mjs verifies classification accuracy across 7 provider error patterns plus false-positive prevention</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Created scripts/check-quota-hint.mjs with 15 test cases covering quota and rate-limit detection</fact>
    <fact>Tests Direct adapter 429 format for Anthropic and OpenAI APIs detecting rate-limited classification</fact>
    <fact>Verifies quota patterns take precedence: 429 with insufficient_quota classified as quota-exhausted not rate-limited</fact>
    <fact>Tests provider-specific patterns: DeepSeek 402 Insufficient Balance, Anthropic credit balance, Anthropic 529 overloaded, Gemini RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED</fact>
